questions.question: What are Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs) and how do they relate to adult attachment?

options.text: Minor childhood disappointments that have no impact on adult relationships
Abuse, neglect, and household dysfunction in childhood that predict insecure adult attachment, which then affects romantic relationships
Only severe physical or sexual abuse — emotional neglect doesn't count
Experiences that guarantee you'll have terrible relationships forever

questions.explanation: ACEs include emotional/physical/sexual abuse, emotional/physical neglect, and household dysfunction (parental substance abuse, mental illness, domestic violence, incarceration, divorce). Research shows ACEs → insecure attachment → relationship impairment is a well-replicated developmental pathway. However, this is probabilistic (not deterministic) — protective factors (one secure caregiver, therapy, secure adult relationships) can buffer the impact.

questions.question: How do specific childhood caregiving patterns shape adult attachment anxiety vs. avoidance?

options.text: All adverse childhood experiences produce identical attachment patterns
Inconsistent caregiver responsiveness → attachment anxiety; emotional neglect/dismissiveness → attachment avoidance; severe abuse/trauma → fearful-avoidant
Childhood has no impact on adult attachment patterns
Only genetics determine attachment, not childhood experiences

questions.explanation: Research shows specific childhood patterns predict specific adult attachment: Inconsistent responsiveness (sometimes available, sometimes not) → child can't predict availability → adult hypervigilance to abandonment (anxiety). Emotional neglect/dismissiveness → child learns vulnerability is unsafe → adult discomfort with intimacy (avoidance). Severe abuse/trauma → caregiver is both source of comfort and threat → adult 'come here, go away' dynamic (fearful-avoidant).

questions.question: What is 'earned security' in attachment research?

options.text: When you pay for therapy to fix your attachment
Adults who experienced insecure childhood attachment but developed secure patterns through later relationships, therapy, or corrective experiences
A myth — childhood attachment never changes
Only possible for people who had perfect childhoods

questions.explanation: Earned security describes adults with insecure childhood attachment who developed secure patterns through significant later experiences: secure adult relationships, therapy, mentors, or other corrective relational experiences. This is distinguished from 'continuous security' (secure in childhood and adulthood). The 2026 systematic review confirms therapy produces meaningful shifts toward security — earned security is real and achievable.

questions.question: When is trauma-focused therapy (EMDR, CPT, PE, AEDP) appropriate instead of or alongside relationship education?

options.text: Never — relationship education alone can address all childhood trauma
When childhood abuse, neglect, or household violence significantly affects current functioning or when exploring early experiences triggers overwhelming emotion or flashbacks
Only if you were diagnosed with PTSD by age 10
Trauma therapy is never relevant to relationship issues

questions.explanation: Trauma-focused therapy is appropriate when childhood abuse, neglect, or household violence significantly affects current functioning, when remembering early experiences triggers flashbacks/dissociation/intense dysregulation, or when you recognize relationship difficulties are rooted in unprocessed trauma. This course is psychoeducational — it cannot provide trauma processing. Evidence-based treatments (EMDR, CPT, PE, AEDP) should precede or accompany relationship education when trauma is present.

questions.question: Which statement reflects the healthiest understanding of childhood's impact on adult attachment?

options.text: My childhood made me this way and I can never change
My childhood shaped my default patterns, but I can build new relational habits through deliberate practice and secure experiences — patterns are not destiny
Childhood has zero impact on adult relationships
I should blame my parents for all my relationship problems

questions.explanation: The evidence-informed understanding: childhood experiences shape internal working models and default attachment patterns, but these are NOT permanent. The 2026 review confirms therapy and secure relationships shift patterns toward security. Acknowledging developmental origins cultivates self-compassion (you understand why patterns exist) while taking responsibility for current change (you're an adult with agency). This honors the past without being imprisoned by it.

title: Attachment and Childhood — Developmental Pathways - Quiz
